comment_text.sn35.74:11.1: The expression rāgavirāga plays on two senses of the word. The root sense of rāga is “dye” or “color”, especially red (cf. rajaka, MN 56:27.14) . From there it took on the sense “greed, lust, passion” which dominates in Pali. Thus rāgavirāga is both the “fading of color” and the “dispassion of passion”. It is a term for the quelling of harmful emotions (AN 2.31).

root_text.sn35.74:1.2: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami …pe… ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.74:1.3: “amukasmiṁ, bhante, vihāre aññataro bhikkhu navo appaññāto ābādhiko dukkhito bāḷhagilāno.
root_text.sn35.74:1.4: Sādhu, bhante, bhagavā yena so bhikkhu tenupasaṅkamatu anukampaṁ upādāyā”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:2.1: Atha kho bhagavā navavādañca sutvā gilānavādañca, “appaññāto bhikkhū”ti iti viditvā yena so bhikkhu tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn35.74:2.2: Addasā kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
root_text.sn35.74:2.4: Atha kho bhagavā taṁ bhikkhuṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.74:2.8: Nisajja kho bhagavā taṁ bhikkhuṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.74:2.9: “kacci te, bhikkhu, khamanīyaṁ, kacci yāpanīyaṁ, kacci dukkhā vedanā paṭikkamanti no abhikkamanti, paṭikkamosānaṁ paññāyati no abhikkamo”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:3.1: “Na me, bhante, khamanīyaṁ, na yāpanīyaṁ, bāḷhā me dukkhā vedanā abhikkamanti no paṭikkamanti, abhikkamosānaṁ paññāyati no paṭikkamo”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:4.1: “Kacci te, bhikkhu, na kiñci kukkuccaṁ, na koci vippaṭisāro”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:5.1: “Taggha me, bhante, anappakaṁ kukkuccaṁ, anappako vippaṭisāro”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:6.1: “Kacci pana taṁ, bhikkhu, attā sīlato upavadatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:7.1: “Na kho maṁ, bhante, attā sīlato upavadatī”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:8.1: “No ce kira te, bhikkhu, attā sīlato upavadati, atha kiñca te kukkuccaṁ ko ca vippaṭisāro”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:9.1: “Na khvāhaṁ, bhante, sīlavisuddhatthaṁ bhagavatā dhammaṁ desitaṁ ājānāmī”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:10.1: “No ce kira tvaṁ, bhikkhu, sīlavisuddhatthaṁ mayā dhammaṁ desitaṁ ājānāsi, atha kimatthaṁ carahi tvaṁ, bhikkhu, mayā dhammaṁ desitaṁ ājānāsī”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:11.1: “Rāgavirāgatthaṁ khvāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā dhammaṁ desitaṁ ājānāmī”ti.
root_text.sn35.74:12.2: Sādhu kho tvaṁ, bhikkhu, rāgavirāgatthaṁ mayā dhammaṁ desitaṁ ājānāsi.
root_text.sn35.74:12.3: Rāgavirāgattho hi, bhikkhu, mayā dhammo desito.
root_text.sn35.74:16.1: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.74:18.1: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, kallaṁ nu taṁ samanupassituṁ:
root_text.sn35.74:20.1: “Evaṁ passaṁ, bhikkhu, sutavā ariyasāvako cakkhusmimpi nibbindati, sotasmimpi nibbindati …pe… manasmimpi nibbindati.
root_text.sn35.74:20.2: Nibbindaṁ virajjati; virāgā vimuccati; vimuttasmiṁ vimuttamiti ñāṇaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn35.74:21.2: Attamano so bhikkhu bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinandi.
root_text.sn35.74:21.3: Imasmiñca pana veyyākaraṇasmiṁ bhaññamāne tassa bhikkhuno virajaṁ vītamalaṁ dhammacakkhuṁ udapādi:

translation_text.sn35.74:1.2: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, and said to him,
translation_text.sn35.74:1.3: “Sir, in such and such a monastery there’s a mendicant who is junior and not well-known. He’s sick, suffering, gravely ill.
translation_text.sn35.74:1.4: Please go to him out of sympathy.”
translation_text.sn35.74:2.1: When the Buddha heard that the mendicant was junior and ill, understanding that he was not well-known, he went to him.
translation_text.sn35.74:2.2: That mendicant saw the Buddha coming off in the distance
translation_text.sn35.74:2.4: The Buddha said to that monk,
translation_text.sn35.74:2.5: “It’s all right, mendicant, don’t get up.
translation_text.sn35.74:2.6: There are some seats spread out, I will sit there.”
translation_text.sn35.74:2.7: He sat on the seat spread out
translation_text.sn35.74:2.9: “I hope you’re keeping well, mendicant; I hope you’re all right. I hope that your pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.”
translation_text.sn35.74:3.1: “Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.”
translation_text.sn35.74:4.1: “I hope you don’t have any remorse or regret?”
translation_text.sn35.74:5.1: “Indeed, sir, I have no little remorse and regret.”
translation_text.sn35.74:6.1: “I hope you have no reason to blame yourself when it comes to ethical conduct?”
translation_text.sn35.74:7.1: “No sir, I have no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct.”
translation_text.sn35.74:8.1: “In that case, mendicant, why do you have remorse and regret?”
translation_text.sn35.74:9.1: “Because I understand that the Buddha has not taught the Dhamma merely for the sake of ethical purity.”
translation_text.sn35.74:10.1: “If that is so, what then do you understand to be the purpose of teaching the Dhamma?”
translation_text.sn35.74:11.1: “I understand that the Buddha has taught the Dhamma for the purpose of the fading away of greed.”
translation_text.sn35.74:12.2: It’s good that you understand that I’ve taught the Dhamma for the purpose of the fading away of greed.
translation_text.sn35.74:12.3: For that is indeed the purpose.
translation_text.sn35.74:12.5: Is the eye permanent or impermanent?”
translation_text.sn35.74:16.1: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn35.74:18.1: “But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus:
translation_text.sn35.74:18.2: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn35.74:20.1: “Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind.
translation_text.sn35.74:20.2: Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed.
translation_text.sn35.74:20.3: They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended … there is nothing further for this place.’”
translation_text.sn35.74:21.1: That is what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn35.74:21.2: Satisfied, that mendicant approved what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn35.74:21.3: And while this discourse was being spoken, the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in that mendicant:
translation_text.sn35.74:21.4: “Everything that has a beginning has an end.”

variant_text.sn35.74:6.1: taṁ → tvaṁ (bj, pts1ed); te (sya-all, km, mr)
variant_text.sn35.74:7.1: sīlato upavadatī”ti → no hetaṁ bhante (pts1ed, mr)
variant_text.sn35.74:8.1: atha kiñca → atha kismiñca (bj); atha bhikkhu kismiñca (sya-all, km, pts1ed, mr)
